content.text: Allow and deny rules are fine-grained entries in Claude Code's settings files that tell the permission gate exactly which tool calls to auto-approve and which to block outright — sitting between the broad permission mode and the individual tool invocation.

Every time Claude Code wants to use a tool — edit a file, run a shell command, fetch a URL, call an MCP server — the request passes through a permission gate before it reaches the system. The gate consults three sources in order: deny rules, allow rules, and the active permission mode. Allow and deny rules are the data-driven layer in that stack, giving you surgical control over individual tools without changing the broad mode.
mark specific tool calls as safe to run automatically. When a tool invocation matches an allow rule (and no deny rule), the gate lets it through without showing a prompt.
mark specific tool calls as forbidden. When a tool invocation matches a deny rule, the gate refuses it immediately — regardless of what allow rules or the permission mode would otherwise say.
is a glob-style pattern matched against the argument passed to that tool. For
this is the command string; for file tools it is the file path.
) matches all invocations of that tool, regardless of argument.
matches any characters within a single path segment or command word;
matches across multiple path segments. For Bash rules, the argument is the full command string, so

An allow rule auto-approves the matching tool call — the permission gate executes it without pausing to prompt. This is the primary tool for eliminating repetitive approval prompts on operations you already trust.
Allow rules work in any interactive permission mode —
mode they have no effect because tools are never executed. In
mode they are redundant because all tool calls are already auto-approved.
A deny rule blocks the matching tool call outright. Claude Code reports that the action was denied and does not execute it. The defining property of deny rules is that
: when a tool call matches both an allow rule and a deny rule, the deny rule takes precedence.
This makes deny rules the right tool for "never do this under any circumstances" constraints — protections you want enforced regardless of the active mode or allow list.
mode, where the permission gate is otherwise permissive. This is one reason the recommended alternative to
combined with a well-defined deny list.
Allow and deny rules are stored in the
key of any settings file in the standard hierarchy:
Personal allow rules for your usual workflow (test runners, safe git ops)
Team-wide deny rules, project-specific allow rules
Experimental rules you don't want to share yet

Rules from all applicable files are merged. When the same tool call matches a rule at both the user level and the project level, deny still takes overall precedence regardless of which file it comes from.
The permission gate applies rules in a fixed sequence on every tool invocation:
If the tool call matches any deny rule, it is refused immediately. No further evaluation.
If the tool call matches any allow rule, it is auto-approved and executes without a prompt.
The active permission mode determines what happens next — prompt the user (
This means allow and deny rules are always consulted
the mode has any say. A well-tuned allow/deny list therefore gives you predictable, deterministic behavior for the commands that matter most, while the mode handles everything else.
Missing the wildcard on Bash rules.

is in the deny list, force-pushes are still blocked. Deny wins — always.
. Keep allow rules narrow; use deny rules to carve out dangerous variants from a wide pattern.
Putting team deny rules only in user settings.
Teammates who don't share your user settings file get no protection. Commit team-wide deny rules in
Forgetting that plan mode ignores allow rules.
In plan mode, tools are never executed, so allow rules have no effect. Switching to plan mode enforces read-only behavior, not security.
Define deny rules for destructive, irreversible, or production-touching commands
you relax the permission mode. Deny rules are your safety net.
Build your allow list incrementally. Run in
mode, observe which safe actions Claude repeats, then add allow rules for them. Don't guess at a broad allow list upfront.
with project-wide deny rules so all contributors share the same protections automatically.
Keep user-level allow rules scoped to tools and commands that are safe across all your projects. A rule that makes sense for one project's test suite might be too permissive in another context.
Pair broad allow rules with targeted deny rules. Allowing
lets read-only and safe git operations through while blocking the dangerous variants.
Allow rules auto-approve matching tool calls, eliminating prompts for repetitive safe operations.
Deny rules block matching tool calls outright; they take precedence over any allow rule or permission mode.
The evaluation order is fixed: deny → allow → mode default.
; omitting the parentheses matches all invocations of that tool.

The recommended approach to reducing prompt fatigue is
mode combined with a carefully curated allow/deny list — not bypassing permissions entirely.
Understanding the internals of allow & deny rules helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with allow & deny rules has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying allow & deny rules in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Allow & Deny Rules is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
Production experience with allow & deny rules has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.

Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.

Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.

Allow & Deny Rules is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Claude Code's permission system uses allow and deny rules to control which tools and operations Claude can execute autonomously. Each rule is a string that matches against tool call signatures using a combination of exact matching and glob patterns. A rule consists of a tool category prefix followed by an optional specific operation or path: 'Bash' matches any Bash command, 'Bash(git *)' matches any Bash command that starts with 'git', and 'Bash(git commit*)' matches only git commit commands. File-system operations use path patterns: 'Read(src/**)' allows reading any file under the src directory, and 'Write(*.json)' allows writing any JSON file in the current directory. The same pattern syntax applies to both allow and deny rules. Rules are written in the permissions array under either 'allow' or 'deny' keys in the settings.json file.

When a tool call matches both an allow rule and a deny rule simultaneously, the deny rule always wins. This is the fundamental safety property of the permission system: explicit denial is stronger than explicit allowance, regardless of specificity or ordering. For example, if you allow 'Bash(git *)' and deny 'Bash(git push*)', a git push command matches both — but the deny rule takes precedence and Claude cannot execute it without explicit user approval. This deny-wins precedence is intentional: it makes it impossible to accidentally grant permission to a dangerous operation by adding an overly broad allow rule. Within the deny rules themselves, the most specific matching rule applies — 'Bash(git push --force*)' is more specific than 'Bash(git push*)'. Within the allow rules, the same specificity principle applies.
Deny wins: when a tool call matches both an allow and a deny rule, the deny rule always takes precedence
No ordering dependency: rule precedence is deny-over-allow, not determined by position in the array
Most specific match: within allow rules only, more specific patterns take precedence over broader patterns
No allow = prompt: if a tool call matches no allow rule and no deny rule, Claude prompts the user for approval
Explicit deny = block: if a tool call matches a deny rule, it is always blocked — no prompt offered
Implicit allow via * wildcard: 'Bash(*)' or 'Bash' allows ALL bash commands — use with extreme caution
Layered evaluation order: deny rules are checked first; only if no deny rule matches are allow rules checked
Scope of Rules — Global vs Project vs User Level

Permission rules exist at three configuration levels, and rules at different levels compose by merging: global rules apply to all projects in all sessions, project rules apply to the current project only, and user-local rules apply to the current user in the current project. Global configuration lives in ~/.claude/settings.json; project configuration lives in .claude/settings.json at the repository root; user-local project configuration lives in .claude/settings.local.json (typically gitignored). When evaluating a tool call, Claude checks deny rules from all three levels first — a deny at any level blocks execution. Then allow rules from all three levels are checked — an allow at any level (combined with no deny) permits execution. This three-level hierarchy enables shared project-level rules for the whole team (in .claude/settings.json committed to git) while allowing individual developers to add their own personal permissions via settings.local.json.

Several permission patterns recur across different types of projects and team compositions. The read-only pattern is used when Claude is acting as a code reviewer or analyst — allow all Read operations, deny all Write and Bash commands. The restricted-write pattern is the most common for development work — allow reads everywhere, allow writes only in src/ and tests/, deny all infrastructure-touching commands. The CI-safe pattern is used when Claude runs in a CI environment — allow the specific commands needed for the workflow (lint, test, build) but deny anything that touches deployment, credentials, or network services. Understanding these patterns lets you choose a starting configuration rather than building rules from scratch.
Read-only: allow Read(**), deny Write(**), deny Bash — safe for code review and documentation workflows
Restricted write: allow Read(**), allow Write(src/** tests/**), deny Write(.env* secrets*) — standard development
Git-safe: allow Bash(git add* git commit* git diff* git log* git status*), deny Bash(git push* git reset*)
CI pipeline: allow specific Bash(npm run lint), Bash(npm run test), Bash(npm run build), deny deployment commands
Admin: allow all with minimal deny rules — for trusted developers who need full system access
Sandbox: deny Bash(*) entirely, allow only Read and mcp tools — for isolated research or documentation tasks
MCP-gated: allow mcp__github__* but deny mcp__github__delete_* and mcp__github__merge_* — GitHub read+write-minus-destructive

Before deploying a permission configuration to a project or team, validate it with targeted tests. The most direct test is to attempt operations that should be denied and verify they trigger a permission prompt rather than executing silently. In Claude Code, a blocked command shows a message indicating the operation requires approval — if you have auto-approve turned off for blocked commands, you should see the denial. For allow rules, verify that the intended operations proceed without prompts. Pay particular attention to edge cases: rules with wildcards may have unexpected matches (does 'Bash(git *)' also match 'Bash(git clean -fd)' — destructive!), and path patterns need testing for both the intended paths and adjacent paths that should not be covered.

# Test allow rules: these should proceed WITHOUT a prompt (with Claude Code running)
# Test: git commands should be allowed
git status
git diff HEAD
git add -p

# Test deny rules: these should be BLOCKED or PROMPTED
# (run these in Claude Code to verify the permission system works)
git push origin main          # should be denied by Bash(git push*)
git reset --hard HEAD~1       # should be denied by Bash(git reset --hard*)
rm -rf node_modules           # should be denied by Bash(rm -rf *)

# Verify edge cases in wildcard rules
# 'Bash(npm run *)' — does it also match 'npm run deploy:production'?
npm run lint        # should be allowed
npm run test        # should be allowed
# If 'npm run deploy:production' is also matched, add an explicit deny:
# "Bash(npm run deploy*)" to the deny list

# Check file write permissions with boundary tests
# Write to allowed path — should succeed
echo 'test' > src/test-file.tmp

# Write to denied path — should be blocked
echo 'SECRET=test' > .env     # should be denied by Write(**/.env*)

The principle of least privilege is the foundational security principle for Claude Code permission configuration: grant only the permissions Claude needs to accomplish its task, and no more. Start with a restrictive configuration and add permissions when you encounter legitimate needs, rather than starting permissive and trying to remove access later. Treat the deny list as your security boundary: it should include all operations that could cause irreversible harm — force-pushes, database drops, production deployments, secret file writes. Gitignore settings.local.json to prevent personal permission expansions from being accidentally shared. For CI environments, create a separate service account with minimal permissions rather than running Claude Code with developer-level access. Audit permission configuration changes through code review the same way you would audit changes to infrastructure-as-code.
Least privilege: grant only what is needed; add permissions when needed rather than starting permissive
Irreversibility test: anything that cannot be undone (force push, DROP TABLE, file deletion) belongs in deny list
Gitignore settings.local.json: prevent accidental sharing of personal permission expansions
Separate CI identity: run Claude Code in CI with a minimal-permission service account, not developer credentials
Code-review permission changes: treat settings.json changes as security-critical infrastructure changes
No wildcard allows without wildcard denies: 'Bash(*)' allow must be paired with explicit denies for destructive commands
Credential file protection: always deny Write(**/.env*), Write(**/secrets*), Write(**/*.pem), Write(**/*.key)
